annotations.label: Matrix

anchor.snippet: a matrix ( pl. : matrices ) is a rectangular array of numbers or other mathematical objects

annotations.note: Mathlib defines `Matrix m n α := m → n → α`, matching the rectangular-array notion with arbitrary entry type.

annotations.label: 2×3 matrix example

anchor.snippet: denotes a matrix with two rows and three columns

annotations.note: A concrete 2×3 example matrix is not part of Mathlib's library.

anchors.snippet: denotes a matrix with two rows and three columns

annotations.label: Square matrix (lead)

anchor.snippet: Square matrices , matrices with the same number of rows and columns

annotations.note: Square matrices are written as `Matrix n n α` (same index type for rows and columns); there is no separate `SquareMatrix` definition.

annotations.label: Invertibility and determinant

anchor.snippet: a square matrix is invertible if and only if it has a nonzero determinant

annotations.note: Mathlib proves `IsUnit A ↔ IsUnit A.det` over a commutative ring; over a field this reduces to det ≠ 0.

annotations.label: Matrix (formal)

anchor.snippet: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers (or other mathematical objects), called the "entries"

annotations.note: Same `Matrix` definition encodes the formal rectangular-array notion.

anchors.snippet: A matrix is a rectangular array of numbers (or other mathematical objects), called the "entries"

annotations.label: Real and complex matrix

anchor.snippet: A real matrix and a complex matrix are matrices whose entries are respectively real numbers or complex numbers

annotations.note: Real/complex matrices are instances `Matrix m n ℝ` / `Matrix m n ℂ`; no dedicated abbreviations.

anchors.snippet: A real matrix and a complex matrix are matrices whose entries are respectively real numbers or complex numbers

annotations.label: Rows and columns

anchor.snippet: The horizontal and vertical lines of entries in a matrix are respectively called rows and columns

annotations.note: Matrices are indexed by row/column types and `Matrix.row`/`Matrix.col` extract row/column functions.

annotations.label: Size of a matrix

anchor.snippet: The size of a matrix is defined by the number of rows and columns it contains

annotations.note: Size is encoded by the index types `m` and `n`; no explicit `size` function is defined.

annotations.label: m × n matrix

anchor.snippet: A matrix with m rows and n columns is called an m × n matrix

annotations.note: Expressed as `Matrix (Fin m) (Fin n) α`; no separate `mxnMatrix` abbreviation.

annotations.label: Row and column vectors

anchor.snippet: Matrices with a single row are called row matrices or row vectors

annotations.note: Row/column vectors are typically written as `Matrix Unit n α` or via `Matrix.row`/`Matrix.col`; no separate named type.

annotations.label: Square matrix

anchor.snippet: A matrix with the same number of rows and columns is called a square matrix

annotations.note: Square matrices are written `Matrix n n α`; no dedicated wrapper type.

annotations.label: Infinite matrix

anchor.snippet: A matrix with an infinite number of rows or columns (or both) is called an infinite matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Matrix m n α` allows arbitrary (including infinite) index types, but many operations (mul, det) require `Fintype`.

annotations.label: Empty matrix

anchor.snippet: it is useful to consider a matrix with no rows or no columns, called an empty matrix

annotations.note: Empty matrices are `Matrix m n α` with `IsEmpty m` or `IsEmpty n`; no dedicated alias.

annotations.label: Matrix addition and subtraction

anchor.snippet: Matrix addition and subtraction require matrices of a consistent size, and are calculated entrywise

annotations.note: Entrywise `Add`, `Sub`, `Neg` instances on `Matrix m n α` are defined inheriting from the pi type.

annotations.label: Commutativity of matrix addition

anchor.snippet: addition is commutative , that is, the matrix sum does not depend on the order of the summands

annotations.note: The `AddCommMonoid (Matrix m n α)` instance gives commutativity of matrix addition.

annotations.label: Scalar multiplication

anchor.snippet: The product c A of a number c (also called a scalar in this context) and a matrix A is computed by multiplying each entry of A by c

annotations.note: Entrywise `SMul R (Matrix m n α)` instance encodes scalar multiplication.

anchors.snippet: The product c A of a number c (also called a scalar in this context) and a matrix A is computed by multiplying each entry of A by c

annotations.label: Transpose

anchor.snippet: The transpose of an m × n matrix A is the n × m matrix A T (also denoted A tr or t A ) formed by turning rows into columns and vice versa

annotations.note: `Matrix.transpose M i j = M j i`, notated `Mᵀ`.

anchors.snippet: The transpose of an m × n matrix A is the n × m matrix A T (also denoted A tr or t A ) formed by turning rows into columns and vice versa

annotations.label: Transpose properties

anchor.snippet: The transpose is compatible with addition and scalar multiplication

annotations.note: `Matrix.transpose_add`, `transpose_smul`, `transpose_mul` are all in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Matrix multiplication

anchor.snippet: If A is an m × n matrix and B is an n × p matrix, then their matrix product AB is the m × p matrix

annotations.note: Matrix multiplication is defined via `HMul`/`Mul` instances on `Matrix m n α` × `Matrix n p α`.

anchors.snippet: If A is an m × n matrix and B is an n × p matrix, then their matrix product AB is the m × p matrix

annotations.label: Associativity and distributivity of matrix multiplication

anchor.snippet: Matrix multiplication satisfies the rules

annotations.note: `Matrix.mul_assoc` and the `NonUnitalNonAssocSemiring`/`Semiring` instances yield distributivity.

anchors.snippet: Matrix multiplication satisfies the rules

annotations.label: Non-commutativity of matrix multiplication

anchor.snippet: matrix multiplication is not commutative , in marked contrast to (rational, real, or complex) numbers

annotations.note: No explicit Mathlib lemma asserting non-commutativity of matrix multiplication (it is implicit in the lack of `CommRing` instance).

anchors.snippet: matrix multiplication is not commutative , in marked contrast to (rational, real, or complex) numbers

annotations.label: Row operations

anchor.snippet: There are three types of row operations

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Matrix.updateRow`, `Matrix.transvection`, and row swapping but no single unified "elementary row operation" classifier.

annotations.label: Submatrix

anchor.snippet: A submatrix of a matrix is a matrix obtained by deleting any collection of rows or columns or both

annotations.note: `Matrix.submatrix A r c` reindexes rows/columns by arbitrary functions, subsuming the row/column-deletion view.

anchors.snippet: A submatrix of a matrix is a matrix obtained by deleting any collection of rows or columns or both

annotations.label: Principal submatrix

anchor.snippet: A principal submatrix is a square submatrix obtained by removing certain rows and columns

annotations.note: Expressible as `M.submatrix f f` but there is no `principalSubmatrix` definition.

annotations.label: Matrix equation for linear systems

anchor.snippet: Matrices can be used to compactly write and work with multiple linear equations

annotations.note: Linear systems are written `A *ᵥ x = b` via `Matrix.mulVec`; no dedicated `LinearSystem` notion.

anchors.snippet: Matrices can be used to compactly write and work with multiple linear equations

annotations.label: Solution via inverse matrix

anchor.snippet: If n = m and the equations are independent , then this can be done by writing

annotations.note: Mathlib provides `A⁻¹` (`nonsing_inv`) and `A * A⁻¹ = 1` results, from which `x = A⁻¹ *ᵥ b` follows, but the explicit system-solution statement is not a single lemma.

anchors.snippet: If n = m and the equations are independent , then this can be done by writing

annotations.label: Linear transformation from matrix

anchor.snippet: A real m -by- n matrix A gives rise to a linear transformation

annotations.note: `Matrix.toLin'` is the linear-equivalence `Matrix m n R ≃ₗ[R] (n → R) →ₗ[R] (m → R)`.

annotations.label: 2×2 matrix as parallelogram transform

anchor.snippet: the 2 × 2 matrix [MATH] can be viewed as the transform of the unit square into a parallelogram

annotations.note: The geometric parallelogram-image example is not part of Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the 2 × 2 matrix [MATH] can be viewed as the transform of the unit square into a parallelogram

annotations.label: Matrix multiplication as composition

anchor.snippet: Under the 1-to-1 correspondence between matrices and linear maps, matrix multiplication corresponds to composition of maps

annotations.note: `LinearMap.toMatrix_comp` (and `toMatrix'_comp`) state that composition of linear maps corresponds to matrix multiplication.

anchors.snippet: Under the 1-to-1 correspondence between matrices and linear maps, matrix multiplication corresponds to composition of maps

annotations.label: Rank of a matrix

anchor.snippet: The rank of a matrix A is the maximum number of linearly independent row vectors of the matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.rank` is defined and `rank_eq_finrank_span_cols`/`rank_eq_finrank_span_row` equate it with row/column rank.

annotations.label: Rank–nullity theorem

anchor.snippet: The rank–nullity theorem states that the dimension of the kernel of a matrix plus the rank equals the number of columns

annotations.note: Rank-nullity is `LinearMap.finrank_range_add_finrank_ker`, used in `Matrix/Rank.lean` for matrices via `toLin`.

annotations.label: Square matrix and main diagonal

anchor.snippet: A square matrix is a matrix with the same number of rows and columns

annotations.note: Square matrices are `Matrix n n α`; main diagonal is `Matrix.diag`.

annotations.label: Square matrices form noncommutative ring

anchor.snippet: Square matrices of a given dimension form a noncommutative ring

annotations.note: `instance instRing : Ring (Matrix n n α)` for `Fintype n` and `Ring α`.

annotations.label: Triangular and diagonal matrices

anchor.snippet: If all entries of A below the main diagonal are zero, A is called an upper triangular matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.BlockTriangular` (and `Matrix.IsDiag` for diagonal) generalize triangular matrices; no specific `IsUpperTriangular` predicate.

annotations.label: Identity matrix

anchor.snippet: The identity matrix I n of size n is the n -by- n matrix in which all the elements on the main diagonal are equal to 1

annotations.note: `instance one : One (Matrix n n α) := ⟨diagonal fun _ => 1⟩` gives the identity matrix `1`.

anchors.snippet: The identity matrix I n of size n is the n -by- n matrix in which all the elements on the main diagonal are equal to 1

annotations.label: Scalar matrix

anchor.snippet: A scalar multiple of an identity matrix is called a scalar matrix

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Matrix.diagonalRingHom`/`scalar` (sending `r` to `r • 1` as a ring hom) but no `IsScalarMatrix` predicate.

annotations.label: Symmetric and skew-symmetric matrices

anchor.snippet: A square matrix A that is equal to its transpose, that is, A = A T , is a symmetric matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.IsSymm A := Aᵀ = A` is defined; no separate `IsSkewSymm` predicate, though `-A = Aᵀ` is expressible.

annotations.label: Hermitian matrix

anchor.snippet: symmetry is often replaced by the concept of Hermitian matrices , which satisfies A ∗ = A

annotations.note: `Matrix.IsHermitian A := Aᴴ = A` is the standard Mathlib definition.

annotations.label: Spectral theorem

anchor.snippet: By the spectral theorem , real symmetric matrices and complex Hermitian matrices have an eigenbasis

annotations.note: `Matrix.IsHermitian.spectral_theorem` together with `eigenvectorBasis` gives the spectral decomposition.

annotations.label: Invertible matrix

anchor.snippet: A square matrix A is called invertible or non-singular if there exists a matrix B such that

annotations.note: Invertibility is `IsUnit A` in the matrix ring; characterized by `isUnit_iff_isUnit_det` and `Matrix.invertibleOfDetInvertible`.

anchors.snippet: A square matrix A is called invertible or non-singular if there exists a matrix B such that

annotations.label: Uniqueness of inverse

anchor.snippet: If B exists, it is unique and is called the inverse matrix of A

annotations.note: Uniqueness follows from `Group`/`Units` structure on the matrix ring; the chosen inverse is `A⁻¹` (`nonsing_inv`).

anchors.snippet: If B exists, it is unique and is called the inverse matrix of A

annotations.label: Positive-definite and indefinite matrices

anchor.snippet: A symmetric real matrix A is called positive-definite if the associated quadratic form

annotations.note: `Matrix.PosDef` (and `PosSemidef`) capture positive-definiteness via the associated quadratic form.

anchors.snippet: A symmetric real matrix A is called positive-definite if the associated quadratic form

annotations.label: Positive-definite iff eigenvalues positive

anchor.snippet: A symmetric matrix is positive-definite if and only if all its eigenvalues are positive

annotations.note: `Matrix.IsHermitian.posDef_iff_eigenvalues_pos` is exactly this iff.

annotations.label: Bilinear form associated to matrix

anchor.snippet: Allowing as input two different vectors instead yields the bilinear form associated to A

annotations.note: `Matrix.toBilin'` is the linear equivalence `Matrix n n R₁ ≃ₗ BilinForm R₁ (n → R₁)`.

anchors.snippet: Allowing as input two different vectors instead yields the bilinear form associated to A

annotations.label: Orthogonal matrix

anchor.snippet: An orthogonal matrix is a square matrix with real entries whose columns and rows are orthogonal unit vectors

annotations.note: `Matrix.orthogonalGroup n R := unitaryGroup n R`; `mem_orthogonalGroup_iff` says `A * Aᵀ = 1`.

anchors.snippet: An orthogonal matrix is a square matrix with real entries whose columns and rows are orthogonal unit vectors

annotations.label: Orthogonal matrix properties

anchor.snippet: An orthogonal matrix A is necessarily invertible

annotations.note: Orthogonal group is a `Group`, so each element is invertible; no dedicated lemma `IsOrthogonal.invertible`.

annotations.label: Special orthogonal matrix

anchor.snippet: A special orthogonal matrix is an orthogonal matrix with determinant +1

annotations.note: `Matrix.specialOrthogonalGroup`, characterized as orthogonal matrices with `det = 1`.

annotations.label: Trace of a matrix

anchor.snippet: The trace , tr( A ) of a square matrix A is the sum of its diagonal entries

annotations.note: `Matrix.trace A := ∑ i, diag A i`.

anchors.snippet: The trace , tr( A ) of a square matrix A is the sum of its diagonal entries

annotations.label: Trace of product independent of order

anchor.snippet: the trace of the product of two matrices is independent of the order of the factors

annotations.note: `Matrix.trace_mul_comm`: `trace (A * B) = trace (B * A)`.

anchors.snippet: the trace of the product of two matrices is independent of the order of the factors

annotations.label: Trace equals trace of transpose

anchor.snippet: the trace of a matrix is equal to that of its transpose

annotations.note: `Matrix.trace_transpose`: `trace Aᵀ = trace A`.

anchors.snippet: the trace of a matrix is equal to that of its transpose

annotations.label: Determinant

anchor.snippet: The determinant of a square matrix A (denoted det( A ) or | A | ) is a number encoding certain properties of the matrix

annotations.note: `Matrix.det` is defined via `detRowAlternating` as the alternating sum over permutations.

annotations.label: Invertibility iff nonzero determinant

anchor.snippet: A matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is nonzero

annotations.note: `isUnit_iff_isUnit_det` over a `CommRing`; for fields `IsUnit ↔ ≠ 0`.

annotations.label: Determinant of 2×2 matrix

anchor.snippet: The determinant of 2 × 2 matrices is given by

annotations.note: `Matrix.det_fin_two A = A 0 0 * A 1 1 - A 0 1 * A 1 0`.

anchors.snippet: The determinant of 2 × 2 matrices is given by

annotations.label: Multiplicativity of determinant

anchor.snippet: The determinant of a product of square matrices equals the product of their determinants

annotations.note: `Matrix.det_mul`: `det (M * N) = det M * det N`.

anchors.snippet: The determinant of a product of square matrices equals the product of their determinants

annotations.label: Laplace expansion

anchor.snippet: the Laplace expansion expresses the determinant in terms of minors

annotations.note: `Matrix.det_succ_row_zero` (and `det_eq_sum_mul_adjugate_row`) give Laplace expansion via minors.

anchors.snippet: the Laplace expansion expresses the determinant in terms of minors

annotations.label: Cramer's rule

anchor.snippet: Determinants can be used to solve linear systems using Cramer's rule

annotations.note: `Matrix.cramer` and related lemmas (`cramer_apply`, `mul_adjugate`) encode Cramer's rule.

anchors.snippet: Determinants can be used to solve linear systems using Cramer's rule

annotations.label: Eigenvalue and eigenvector

anchor.snippet: are called an eigenvalue and an eigenvector of A , respectively

annotations.note: `Module.End.HasEigenvalue` and `HasEigenvector` are defined for endomorphisms; applied to matrices via `Matrix.toLin`.

anchors.snippet: are called an eigenvalue and an eigenvector of A , respectively

annotations.label: Characteristic polynomial

anchor.snippet: is called the characteristic polynomial of A

annotations.note: `Matrix.charpoly M := det (charmatrix M)`.

anchors.snippet: is called the characteristic polynomial of A

annotations.label: Cayley–Hamilton theorem

anchor.snippet: According to the Cayley–Hamilton theorem , p A ( A ) = 0

annotations.note: `Matrix.aeval_self_charpoly M : aeval M M.charpoly = 0` is Cayley–Hamilton.

anchors.snippet: According to the Cayley–Hamilton theorem , p A ( A ) = 0

annotations.label: Strassen algorithm complexity

anchor.snippet: The Strassen algorithm outperforms this "naive" algorithm; it needs only n 2.807 multiplications

annotations.note: The Strassen matrix-multiplication algorithm and its complexity are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Sparse matrix

anchor.snippet: An important case concerns sparse matrices , that is, matrices whose entries are mostly zero

annotations.note: No notion of sparse matrices is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: LU decomposition

anchor.snippet: The LU decomposition factors matrices as a product of lower ( L ) and an upper triangular matrices ( U )

annotations.note: LU decomposition is not in Mathlib (though `Analysis/Matrix/LDL.lean` covers a related LDL decomposition for positive-definite matrices).

annotations.label: Singular value decomposition

anchor.snippet: Singular value decomposition (SVD) expresses any matrix A as a product UDV ∗

annotations.note: SVD is not formalized for matrices in Mathlib (only related notions like singular values for compact operators).

annotations.label: Eigendecomposition / diagonalization

anchor.snippet: The eigendecomposition or diagonalization expresses A as a product VDV −1

annotations.note: Hermitian diagonalization is covered by `spectral_theorem`; general eigendecomposition is not a single statement.

anchors.snippet: The eigendecomposition or diagonalization expresses A as a product VDV −1

annotations.label: Jordan decomposition

anchor.snippet: the Jordan decomposition transforms a matrix into Jordan normal form

annotations.note: Jordan normal form is not formalized in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: the Jordan decomposition transforms a matrix into Jordan normal form

annotations.label: Matrix ring

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a field or ring
anchor.snippet: is a ring called matrix ring , isomorphic to the endomorphism ring of the left R - module R n

annotations.note: `Ring (Matrix n n α)` instance; isomorphism to endomorphisms is `LinearMap.toMatrixAlgEquiv`.

annotations.label: Invertibility over commutative ring

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a field or ring
anchor.snippet: such a matrix is invertible if and only if its determinant is invertible in R

annotations.note: Exactly `Matrix.isUnit_iff_isUnit_det`.

annotations.label: Supermatrices

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a field or ring
anchor.snippet: Matrices over superrings are called supermatrices

annotations.note: Supermatrices and superrings are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Block matrices

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a field or ring
anchor.snippet: One special but common case is block matrices , which may be considered as matrices whose entries themselves are matrices

annotations.note: `Matrix.fromBlocks` assembles a block matrix from four submatrices, with `toBlocks₁₁`/etc. extracting blocks.

annotations.label: Linear maps as matrices via bases

anchor.snippet: any linear map f : V → W between finite- dimensional vector spaces can be described by a matrix

annotations.note: `LinearMap.toMatrix` is the linear equivalence `(M₁ →ₗ[R] M₂) ≃ₗ Matrix m n R` given bases.

anchors.snippet: any linear map f : V → W between finite- dimensional vector spaces can be described by a matrix

annotations.label: Equivalence of categories

anchor.snippet: the category of matrices with entries in a field [MATH] with multiplication as composition is equivalent to the category of finite-dimensional vector spaces

annotations.note: Mathlib has `LinearMap.toMatrix` but no categorical equivalence between a "matrix category" and finite-dimensional vector spaces.

annotations.label: Matrix group

anchor.snippet: A group in which the objects are invertible ⁠ [MATH] ⁠ matrices and the group operation is matrix multiplication is called a matrix group

annotations.note: Matrix groups are subgroups of `Matrix.GeneralLinearGroup`; no abstract `MatrixGroup` class.

annotations.label: General linear group

anchor.snippet: the general linear group of degree

annotations.note: `Matrix.GeneralLinearGroup n R := (Matrix n n R)ˣ`, with notation `GL n R`.

annotations.label: Special linear group

anchor.snippet: form a group called the special linear group of degree

annotations.note: `Matrix.SpecialLinearGroup n R` is matrices with determinant 1.

anchors.snippet: form a group called the special linear group of degree

annotations.label: Orthogonal group

anchor.snippet: The set of orthogonal matrices , determined by the condition

annotations.note: `Matrix.orthogonalGroup n R := unitaryGroup n R`, satisfying `A * Aᵀ = 1`.

anchors.snippet: The set of orthogonal matrices , determined by the condition

annotations.label: Special orthogonal group

anchor.snippet: Orthogonal matrices with determinant 1 form a group called the special orthogonal group

annotations.note: `Matrix.specialOrthogonalGroup` is orthogonal matrices with `det = 1`.

anchors.snippet: Orthogonal matrices with determinant 1 form a group called the special orthogonal group

annotations.label: Every finite group is a matrix group

anchor.snippet: Every finite group is isomorphic to a matrix group

annotations.note: Cayley's theorem (finite group → permutation group) is in Mathlib, but the explicit composition with the permutation-matrix embedding into `GL n R` is not stated.

annotations.label: Infinite matrices

anchor.snippet: It is also possible to consider matrices with infinitely many rows and columns

annotations.note: `Matrix m n α` allows arbitrary index types so infinite matrices exist, but most operations require `Fintype`.

annotations.label: Finitary matrices

anchor.snippet: An easy way to circumvent this issue is to restrict to finitary matrices

annotations.note: No `FinitaryMatrix` notion in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Empty matrix (formal)

anchor.snippet: An empty matrix is a matrix in which the number of rows or columns (or both) is zero

annotations.note: Empty matrices are `Matrix m n α` with `IsEmpty m ∨ IsEmpty n`; no separate `EmptyMatrix` definition.

annotations.label: Empty matrix product

anchor.snippet: if A is a 3 × 0 matrix and B is a 0 × 3 matrix, then AB is the 3 × 3 zero matrix

annotations.note: Follows directly from `Matrix.mul_apply` with `Fintype.sum_empty`, but no dedicated lemma.

annotations.label: Determinant of 0×0 matrix

anchor.snippet: The determinant of the 0 × 0 matrix is conventionally defined to be 1

annotations.note: `Matrix.det_isEmpty`: when the index type is empty, `det A = 1`.

annotations.label: Semiring

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a semiring
anchor.snippet: A semiring is similar to a ring, but elements need not have additive inverses

annotations.note: `Semiring` is a standard Mathlib type class.

annotations.label: Matrices over semiring form commutative monoid

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a semiring
anchor.snippet: Matrices of fixed size with entries in a semiring form a commutative monoid

annotations.note: `AddCommMonoid (Matrix m n α)` follows from `AddCommMonoid α`, which a semiring provides.

annotations.label: Square matrices over semiring form a semiring

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a semiring
anchor.snippet: Square matrices of fixed size with entries in a semiring form a semiring

annotations.note: `instance semiring : Semiring (Matrix n n α)` for `Fintype n`, `DecidableEq n`, and `Semiring α`.

annotations.label: Positive and negative determinants in a semiring

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a semiring
anchor.snippet: What plays its role instead is the pair of positive and negative determinants

annotations.note: Positive/negative determinant pair for semiring matrices is not formalized.

annotations.label: Bicategory of matrices

anchor.section: Matrices with entries in a category
anchor.snippet: These are part of a bigger structure called the bicategory of matrices

annotations.note: No bicategory-of-matrices construction in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Stochastic matrix

anchor.snippet: Stochastic matrices are square matrices whose rows are probability vectors

annotations.note: `Matrix.rowStochastic` (and `colStochastic`, `doublyStochastic`) capture stochastic matrices.

annotations.label: Random matrices

anchor.snippet: Random matrices are matrices whose entries are random numbers

annotations.note: Random matrices as such are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Adjacency matrix

anchor.snippet: The adjacency matrix of a finite graph is a basic notion of graph theory

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.adjMatrix G α` is the adjacency matrix valued in `α`.

annotations.label: Logical matrix

anchor.snippet: Matrices containing just two different values

annotations.note: Logical (Boolean) matrices are not a named concept in Mathlib (just `Matrix m n Bool`).

annotations.label: Distance matrix

anchor.snippet: The distance (or cost) matrix contains information about the distances of the edges

annotations.note: Distance matrix of a graph is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hessian matrix

anchor.snippet: The Hessian matrix of a differentiable function

annotations.note: No `Hessian` matrix definition in Mathlib (only iterated `fderiv`).

anchors.snippet: The Hessian matrix of a differentiable function

annotations.label: Local minimum via positive-definite Hessian

anchor.snippet: the function has a local minimum if the Hessian matrix is positive definite

annotations.note: Second-derivative test for local minima via positive-definite Hessian is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Jacobi matrix

anchor.snippet: Another matrix frequently used in geometrical situations is the Jacobi matrix of a differentiable map

annotations.note: No standalone `JacobianMatrix` definition in Mathlib's analysis library; only `fderiv` (a continuous linear map).

anchors.snippet: Another matrix frequently used in geometrical situations is the Jacobi matrix of a differentiable map

anchors.value: {\displaystyle J(f)=\left[{\frac {\partial f_{i}}{\partial x_{j}}}\right]_{1\leq i\leq m,1\leq j\leq n}.}

annotations.label: Implicit function theorem application

anchor.snippet: f is locally invertible at that point, by the implicit function theorem

annotations.note: `HasStrictFDerivAt.implicitFunction` formalizes the implicit function theorem (with companion inverse-function-theorem results).

anchors.snippet: f is locally invertible at that point, by the implicit function theorem

anchors.value: {\displaystyle J(f)=\left[{\frac {\partial f_{i}}{\partial x_{j}}}\right]_{1\leq i\leq m,1\leq j\leq n}.}
